analogy: Think of "Land Resources and Agriculture" through the chapter's own opening images: Unit III Unit III Unit III Unit III Unit III Chapter 5 MINERAL AND ENERGY RESOURCES India is endowed with a rich variety of mineral resources due to its varied geological structure. Bulk of the valuable minerals are products of pre-palaezoic age (Refer: Chapter 2... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: MINERAL AND, ENERGY, RESOURCES, 54 India : People and Economy. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Land Resources and Agriculture is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that unit III Unit III Unit III Unit III Unit III Chapter 5 MINERAL AND ENERGY RESOURCES India is endowed with a rich variety of mineral resources due to its varied geological structure.

The text explains that bulk of the valuable minerals are products of pre-palaezoic age (Refer: Chapter 2 of Class XI, Textbook: “Fundamentals of Physical Geography” and are mainly associated with metamorphic and igneous rocks of the peninsular India.

The text explains that the vast alluvial plain tract of north India is devoid of minerals of economic use.

The text explains that the mineral resources provide the country with the necessary base for industrial development.

The text explains that in this chapter, we shall discuss the availability of various types of mineral and energy resources in the country.

The text explains that a mineral is a natural substance of organic or inorganic origin with definite chemical and physical properties.

The text explains that t T T T Types of ypes of ypes of ypes of ypes of Miner Miner Miner Miner Mineral R al R al R al R al Resour esour esour esour esources ces ces ces ces On the basis of chemical and physical properties, minerals may be grouped under two main categories of metallics and non-metallics which may further be classified as follows : Fig.

The text explains that 54 India : People and Economy As, it is clear from the Fig.

The text explains that 5.1 metallic minerals are the sources of metals.

The text explains that iron ore, copper, gold produce metal and are included in this category.

The text explains that metallic minerals are further divided into ferrous and non-ferrous metallic minerals.

The text explains that all those minerals which have iron content are ferrous such as iron ore itself and those which do not have iron content are non-ferrous such as copper, bauxite, etc.

The text explains that non-metallic minerals are either organic in origin such as fossil fuels also known as mineral fuels which are derived from the buried animal and plant life such as coal and petroleum.

The text explains that other type of non-metallic minerals are inorganic in origin such as mica, limestone and graphite, etc.

The text explains that there is inverse relationship in quality and quantity of minerals i.e. good quality minerals are less in quantity as compared to low q u a l i t y m i n e r a l s .

The text explains that t h e t h i r d m a i n characteristic is that all minerals are exhaustible over time.

The text explains that distribution of Minerals in India Distribution of Minerals in India Distribution of Minerals in India Distribution of Minerals in India Distribution of Minerals in India Most of the metallic minerals in India occur in the peninsular plateau region in the old crystalline rocks.

The text explains that most of the major mineral resources occur to the east of a line linking Mangaluru and Kanpur.

The text explains that minerals are generally concentrated in three broad belts in India.

The text explains that have you ever thought about the reason of major iron and steel industry being located in this region?

The text explains that it has variety of minerals viz. iron ore coal, manganese, bauxite, mica.

The text explains that find out the specific region where these minerals are being extracted.

The text explains that this belt does not have as diversified mineral deposits as the north-eastern belt.

The text explains that the North-Western Region This belt extends along Aravali in Rajasthan and part of Gujarat and minerals are associated with Dharwar system of rocks.

The text explains that gypsum and Fuller’s earth deposits are also extensive.

The text explains that you may be knowing that Gujarat and Rajasthan both have rich sources of salt.

The text explains that the Himalayan belt is another mineral belt where copper, lead, zinc, cobalt and tungsten are known to occur.

The text explains that mineral and Energy Resources 55 mineral oil deposits.

The text explains that besides oil resources are also found in off-shore-areas near Mumbai Coast (Mumbai High).

The text explains that in the following pages you will find the spatial pattern of some of the important minerals.

The text explains that ferrous Mineral F e r r o u s m i n e r a l s s u c h a s i r o n o r e , manganese, chromite, etc., provide a strong base for the development of metallurgical industries.

The text explains that our country is well-placed in respect of ferrous minerals both in reserves and production.

The text explains that iron Ore India is endowed with fairly abundant resources of iron ore.

The text explains that manganese deposits are found in almost all geological formations, however, it is mainly associated with Dharwar system.

The text explains that mineral and Energy Resources 57 The manganese belt of Madhya Pradesh extends in a belt in Balaghat-Chhindwara- Nimar-Mandla and Jhabua districts.

The text explains that non-Ferrous Minerals India is poorly endowed with non-ferrous metallic minerals except bauxite.

The text explains that bauxite is found mainly in tertiary deposits and is associated with laterite rocks occurring extensively either on the plateau or hill ranges of peninsular India and also in the coastal tracts of the country.

The text explains that export of strategic and scarce minerals must be reduced, so that the existing reserve may be used for a longer period.

The text explains that choose the right answers of the following from the given options.

The text explains that (i) In which one of the following States are the major oil fields located?

The text explains that (a) Assam (c) Rajasthan (b) Bihar (d) Tamil Nadu (ii) At which one of the following places was the first atomic power station started?

The text explains that (a) Kalpakkam (c) Rana Pratap Sagar (b) Narora (d) Tarapur (iii) Which one of the following is non-renewable source of energy?

The text explains that (a) Hydel (c) Thermal (b) Solar (d) Wind power

The text explains that (i) Give an account of the distribution of mica in India.

The text explains that mention the important nuclear power stations in India.

The text explains that (iv) What are non-conventional sources of energy?

The text explains that (i) Write a detailed note on the Petroleum resources of India.

Source: NCERT Class 12 India People and Economy, Chapter 5: Land Resources and Agriculture
